content:
5 min read
Just now

--

Learn how Android applications communicate with hardware, explore the Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL), HIDL, AIDL, Binder IPC, and build your own simple HAL service on an Android emulator.

Introduction
When you tap the camera icon on your Android phone, something incredible happens in just a fraction of a second.
Camera App
↓
Android Framework
↓
Camera Service
↓
Camera HAL
↓
Linux Kernel Driver
↓
Camera SensorThe same flow occurs when:

• You unlock your phone using a fingerprint.

• Play music through the speakers.

• Turn on the flashlight.

• Record audio.

• Read data from the accelerometer.

• Connect to Wi-Fi or Bluetooth.
But here’s the interesting question:
How does Android support millions of different hardware components from Qualcomm, MediaTek, Samsung, Google, Sony, Xiaomi, and hundreds of other vendors without rewriting Android every time?
The answer is:
HAL — Hardware Abstraction Layer
HAL is one of Android’s most important architectural components.
It separates:
Android Software
↓
Hardwaremaking Android portable across thousands of devices.
In this article you’ll learn:

• What HAL is

• Why Android needs HAL

• Android architecture

• Binder IPC

• HIDL vs AIDL

• Vendor partitions

• Linux kernel drivers

• Hands-on labs using ADB

• Inspecting HAL services

• Building a simple HAL example

• Debugging HAL communication
Let’s begin.

The Problem HAL Solves
Imagine Android without HAL.
Every camera would require different code.
Samsung:
Camera API
↓
Samsung Camera DriverGoogle:
Camera API
↓
Google DriverQualcomm:
Camera API
↓
Qualcomm DriverEvery application would need to understand every hardware vendor.
Impossible.

Enter HAL
HAL creates one common interface.
Applications always communicate with Android.
Android communicates with HAL.
HAL communicates with hardware.
Visualization:
Application
↓
Framework API
↓
Android Service
↓
HAL
↓
Kernel Driver
↓
HardwareApplications never know which hardware exists.

Android Software Stack
The Android architecture consists of several layers.
+--------------------------------+
| Applications |
+--------------------------------+
| Android Framework |
+--------------------------------+
| ART + Native Libraries |
+--------------------------------+
| Native System Services |
+--------------------------------+
| HAL |
+--------------------------------+
| Linux Kernel |
+--------------------------------+
| Hardware |
+--------------------------------+Each layer has a specific responsibility.

Layer 1 — Applications
Examples:

• Camera

• Gallery

• Settings

• Maps

• Chrome
Applications call framework APIs.
Example:
cameraManager.openCamera(...)Notice:
The application never accesses hardware directly.

Layer 2 — Android Framework
The framework contains Java/Kotlin APIs.
Examples:
CameraManager
AudioManager
BluetoothManager
SensorManager
FingerprintManagerFramework components communicate with system services.

ART + Native Libs
ART provides several key services:

• Executes Java/Kotlin bytecode

• Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation

• Ahead-of-Time (AOT) compilation

• Garbage Collection (GC)

• Memory management

• Class loading

• Thread management

• Exception handling
Without ART, Java/Kotlin Android apps would not run.
Native Libs like,
Webkit, OpenMAX AL, Libc, Media Framework, OpenGL ES.

Layer 3 — Native System Services
Examples include:
CameraService
AudioFlinger
SurfaceFlinger
MediaServer
Bluetooth ServiceThese services are written mostly in C++.
They interact with HAL implementations.

Layer 4 — HAL
HAL is where hardware-specific logic begins.
Examples:
Camera HAL
Audio HAL
GPS HAL
Bluetooth HAL
Biometric HAL
Light HAL
USB HALEach hardware vendor provides its own implementation.

Layer 5 — Linux Kernel
The kernel provides:

• Device drivers

• Memory management

• Scheduler

• USB subsystem

• Power management

• Networking
HAL communicates with kernel drivers.

Hands-On Lab 1 — Explore Android Properties
Connect an Android device or emulator:
adb devicesVerify:
adb shellCheck Android version:
getprop ro.build.version.releaseCheck vendor:
getprop ro.product.manufacturerObserve:
Google
Samsung
Xiaomi
MotorolaDifferent hardware.
Same Android framework.

Hands-On Lab 2 — Explore HAL Services
Inside ADB shell:
lshalExample output:
android.hardware.audio
android.hardware.camera
android.hardware.gnss
android.hardware.light
android.hardware.bluetoothThese are registered HAL implementations.
Each one provides hardware functionality.

What is lshal?
lshal stands for:
List HAL ServicesIt shows:

• Interface name

• Version

• Transport

• Implementation
This is one of the best tools for learning Android internals.

HIDL and AIDL
Android has evolved over time.
Older Android versions used:
HIDL(HAL Interface Definition Language)
Modern Android increasingly uses:
Stable AIDL(Android Interface Definition Language)
Both define:
“How Android communicates with HAL.”

Binder IPC
Framework and HAL usually run in different processes.
How do they communicate?
Using:
Binder
Binder is Android’s high-performance IPC mechanism.
Visualization:
Framework
↓
Binder Driver
↓
HAL ServiceBinder is implemented inside the Linux kernel.

Hands-On Lab 3 — Explore Binder
Inside ADB shell:
service listYou’ll see services such as:
activity
package
battery
camera
display
sensorserviceThese communicate using Binder.

Vendor Partition
Modern Android separates the operating system into:
/system
/vendor
/productHAL implementations typically reside in:
/vendorThis allows Android framework updates without replacing vendor drivers.

Hands-On Lab 4 — Explore Vendor Libraries
Inside ADB shell:
ls /vendor/lib64/hwYou may see files such as:
audio.primary.so
camera.vendor.so
lights.default.so
gps.default.soThese are HAL libraries.
Different devices contain different implementations.

Camera HAL Example
Application:
Camera App↓
Framework:
CameraManager↓
Native:
CameraService↓
HAL:
Camera HAL↓
Kernel:
V4L2 Driver↓
Sensor
Every picture follows this path.

Hands-On Lab 5 — Inspect Camera Information
Run:
adb shell dumpsys media.cameraObserve:

• Camera IDs

• Capabilities

• Active devices
This information originates from the camera service and HAL.

Audio HAL
Playing music follows a similar flow.
Spotify
↓
AudioManager
↓
AudioFlinger
↓
Audio HAL
↓
ALSA Driver
↓
SpeakerAndroid never writes directly to the hardware.

Hands-On Lab 6 — Explore Audio
Run:
adb shell dumpsys media.audio_flingerObserve:

• Audio devices

• Mixer threads

• Output streams
This demonstrates the interaction between framework services and the audio HAL.

Writing a Simple HAL (Conceptual)
A HAL interface typically defines operations such as:
interface ILight {
setBrightness(int level);
}A vendor implementation provides:
setBrightness(...)internally.
The framework calls the interface.
The vendor supplies the implementation.
This separation allows different hardware to work with the same Android framework.

Debugging HALs
Useful commands:
View logs:
adb logcatFilter by tag:
adb logcat | grep CameraView system services:
adb shell service listView HAL services:
adb shell lshalView camera service:
adb shell dumpsys media.cameraView audio:
adb shell dumpsys media.audio_flingerThese tools provide excellent insight into Android’s hardware stack.

HAL vs Linux Driver
Many beginners confuse them.
Linux Driver:
Runs in kernel
Controls hardware registers
Handles interruptsHAL:
Runs in user space
Provides standard interface
Calls kernel driverDifferent responsibilities.

Security
HALs run with carefully defined permissions.
Android isolates components using:

• SELinux

• Binder permissions

• Process isolation

• Sandboxing
Applications cannot directly access hardware.
Requests flow through framework services.

Mini Project
Using an Android emulator or development device:

• Connect using ADB.

• Run lshal.

• Run service list.

• Inspect /vendor/lib64/hw.

• Run dumpsys media.camera.

• Run dumpsys media.audio_flinger.

• Compare the available HALs with the physical hardware on your device.
You’ll start recognizing how Android maps hardware features into reusable interfaces.

Common Beginner Misconceptions
“HAL is inside the Linux kernel.”
No.
HAL usually runs in user space.

“Applications call hardware directly.”
No.
They communicate through framework APIs.

“Every phone uses the same camera driver.”
No.
Drivers differ.
HAL hides those differences.

“Binder is part of Java.”
No.
Binder is implemented in the Linux kernel and exposed to Android userspace.

The Big Mental Model
Application
↓
Android Framework API
↓
System Service
↓
Binder IPC
↓
HAL
↓
Linux Kernel Driver
↓
HardwareEvery major hardware feature on Android follows this layered design.

Final Thoughts
The Android Hardware Abstraction Layer is one of the key reasons Android can run on an enormous variety of devices.
Whether your phone contains hardware from Qualcomm, Samsung, MediaTek, Sony, or another vendor, applications continue to use the same Android APIs because HAL standardizes the communication between software and hardware.
Once you understand HAL, Binder, system services, and the Linux kernel beneath them, you stop seeing Android as “just a phone operating system.”
Instead, you begin to appreciate it as a carefully layered architecture that cleanly separates applications, operating system services, vendor implementations, and hardware — one of the reasons Android has scaled to billions of devices worldwide.
